title: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a

items.subtopic: Reduced myocardial perfusion | Cardiac conditions | Systemic conditions | Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome | MEDICAL TREATMENT | ANTI-ISCHEMIC TREATMENT (TABLE 285-3)
items.title: Management of NSTE-ACS: Clinical Monitoring and Treatment
items.content: Patients with a low likelihood of ischemia in NSTE-ACS can be managed in an emergency department or chest pain unit. They require clinical monitoring for recurrent ischemic discomfort, continuous ECGs, and serial hsTn measurements at presentation, 1 hour, and if necessary, 3 hours. For diagnostic uncertainty or intermediate-risk patients, stress testing or cardiac CT angiography may be considered.
keyPoints: Clinical monitoring includes recurrent ischemic discomfort and continuous ECGs.
Serial hsTn monitoring at presentation, 1 h, and if needed, 3 h.
Stress testing or cardiac CT angiography for diagnostic uncertainty or intermediate-risk patients.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · Reduced myocardial perfusion | Cardiac conditions | Systemic conditions | Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome | MEDICAL TREATMENT | ANTI-ISCHEMIC TREATMENT (TABLE 285-3)

items.subtopic: Reduced myocardial perfusion | Cardiac conditions | Systemic conditions | Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome | MEDICAL TREATMENT | ANTI-ISCHEMIC TREATMENT (TABLE 285-3)
items.title: Anti-ischemic Treatment in NSTE-ACS
items.content: Initial anti-ischemic treatment for NSTE-ACS includes nitrates, beta blockers, and oxygen. Nitrates like nitroglycerin are given sublingually or by buccal spray initially, with intravenous administration if symptoms persist. Beta blockers reduce myocardial oxygen demand but should be avoided in acute heart failure or hypotension.
keyPoints: Initial treatment includes nitrates, beta blockers, and oxygen for hypoxemia (arterial O2 saturation <90%).
Nitroglycerin: sublingual/buccal spray initially; intravenous if symptoms persist after three doses 5 min apart.
Beta blockers reduce myocardial oxygen demand but contraindicated in acute heart failure or hypotension.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · Reduced myocardial perfusion | Cardiac conditions | Systemic conditions | Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome | MEDICAL TREATMENT | ANTI-ISCHEMIC TREATMENT (TABLE 285-3)

items.subtopic: Reduced myocardial perfusion | Cardiac conditions | Systemic conditions | Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome | MEDICAL TREATMENT | ANTI-ISCHEMIC TREATMENT (TABLE 285-3)
items.title: Long-term Management and Secondary Prevention in NSTE-ACS
items.content: Long-term management of NSTE-ACS involves therapies to prevent disease progression and recurrent events. This includes early administration of statins, ACE inhibitors or ARBs, and possibly ezetimibe or PCSK9 inhibitors if LDL-C reduction is inadequate.
keyPoints: Therapies focus on preventing disease progression and recurrent NSTE-ACS.
Early intensive statin therapy (e.g., atorvastatin 80 mg/d) reduces ischemic events.
Addition of ezetimibe or PCSK9 inhibitors if LDL-C reduction is <50%.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · Reduced myocardial perfusion | Cardiac conditions | Systemic conditions | Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome | MEDICAL TREATMENT | ANTI-ISCHEMIC TREATMENT (TABLE 285-3)

items.title: Rationale and Dosage for Initial Antiplatelet Therapy in NSTE-ACS
items.content: Initial treatment of patients with Non-ST-Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome (NSTE-ACS) should begin with aspirin, followed by a P2Y12 receptor blocker. Aspirin is administered at 150–325 mg orally or 75–250 mg intravenously initially, then reduced to 75–100 mg daily for maintenance.
keyPoints: Aspirin dose: Initial 150–325 mg oral non-enteric-coated or 75–250 mg IV; Maintenance 75–100 mg/d
Contraindications for aspirin include severe active bleeding and aspirin allergy.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Benefits and Risks of Dual Antiplatelet Therapy with Clopidogrel in NSTE-ACS
items.content: Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) using clopidogrel and aspirin reduces cardiovascular death, MI, or stroke by 20% but increases major bleeding risk by 1%. Clopidogrel's loading dose is 300–600 mg, with a maintenance dose of 75 mg daily.
keyPoints: Clopidogrel loading dose: 300–600 mg
Maintenance dose: 75 mg/d
DAPT reduces cardiovascular events by 20% but increases major bleeding risk by an absolute 1%
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Considerations for Prasugrel and Ticagrelor in NSTE-ACS
items.content: Prasugrel and ticagrelor are superior to clopidogrel in preventing recurrent ischemic events but increase bleeding risk. Prasugrel is contraindicated in patients with prior stroke or high bleeding risk, while ticagrelor can be used regardless of management strategy.
keyPoints: Prasugrel loading dose: 60 mg; Maintenance dose: 10 mg/d
Ticagrelor loading dose: 180 mg; Maintenance dose: 90 mg bid
Both prasugrel and ticagrelor increase bleeding risk compared to clopidogrel
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Choosing Anticoagulants for NSTE-ACS
items.content: In managing Non-ST-Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome (NSTE-ACS), selecting the appropriate anticoagulant is crucial. Unfractionated heparin (UFH) and low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) like enoxaparin are commonly used, with enoxaparin showing superiority in reducing recurrent cardiac events but with a modest increase in bleeding. Bivalirudin, a direct thrombin inhibitor, is effective during percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI), while fondaparinux offers similar efficacy to enoxaparin but with reduced major bleeding risk.
keyPoints: Enoxaparin reduces recurrent cardiac events more effectively than UFH in conservatively managed NSTE-ACS patients.
Bivalirudin is used during PCI and has comparable efficacy to UFH or LMWH.
Fondaparinux has a lower risk of major bleeding compared to enoxaparin but requires supplemental UFH for procedure-related thrombosis prevention.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Managing Antithrombotic Therapy in NSTE-ACS with Atrial Fibrillation
items.content: For patients with NSTE-ACS and atrial fibrillation undergoing PCI, dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) duration should be minimized. Aspirin is typically stopped after hospital discharge or within 1 month post-PCI unless the patient has a very high ischemic risk. A P2Y12 inhibitor combined with a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) is continued for 1 year, after which most patients transition to oral anticoagulation monotherapy.
keyPoints: Shorten DAPT duration in NSTE-ACS patients with atrial fibrillation undergoing PCI.
Stop aspirin post-discharge or at most 1 month after PCI unless high ischemic risk is present.
Continue P2Y12 inhibitor plus DOAC for 1 year, then transition to oral anticoagulation monotherapy.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: INVASIVE VERSUS CONSERVATIVE STRATEGY (FIG. 285-4) | LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT
items.title: When to Choose Invasive Strategy in NSTE-ACS
items.content: In patients with non-ST-elevation acute coronary syndrome (NSTE-ACS), an invasive strategy is recommended for those who are unstable or high-risk stable patients. This involves performing coronary arteriography within 2 hours of presentation for unstable patients and within 24 hours for high-risk stable ones, followed by revascularization based on coronary anatomy.
keyPoints: Invasive strategy benefits high-risk NSTE-ACS patients with clinical risk factors, ST-segment deviation, or positive biomarkers.
Immediate invasive strategy (median intervention time of 1.4 and 4.7 hours) reduces death or new MI compared to delayed strategy (median time of 61 and 62 hours).
In low-risk patients, outcomes are similar between invasive and conservative strategies.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · INVASIVE VERSUS CONSERVATIVE STRATEGY (FIG. 285-4) | LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: INVASIVE VERSUS CONSERVATIVE STRATEGY (FIG. 285-4) | LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT
items.title: Long-term Management of NSTE-ACS: Risk Factor Modification
items.content: During hospital discharge for NSTE-ACS, emphasize risk factor modification including smoking cessation, diet, weight management, exercise, and controlling LDL-C to <55 mg/dL, blood pressure, and hyperglycemia.
keyPoints: Optimal long-term therapy includes beta blockers, high-intensity statins (with ezetimibe or PCSK9 inhibitors if needed), ACE inhibitors or ARBs, and SGLT2 or GLP-1 receptor agonists for selected diabetic patients.
Antiplatelet regimen: low-dose aspirin with a P2Y12 inhibitor (prasugrel or ticagrelor) for 12 months unless high bleeding risk; then continue single antiplatelet therapy.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · INVASIVE VERSUS CONSERVATIVE STRATEGY (FIG. 285-4) | LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: INVASIVE VERSUS CONSERVATIVE STRATEGY (FIG. 285-4) | LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT
items.title: DAPT Duration and De-escalation in NSTE-ACS
items.content: For patients not on anticoagulation but at high bleeding risk, shorter DAPT duration of 3–6 months is supported by guidelines. Options include stopping aspirin after this period or de-escalating P2Y12 intensity to clopidogrel while continuing aspirin.
keyPoints: Shorter-duration DAPT (3–6 months) recommended for high bleeding risk, low ischemic event risk patients; then stop aspirin and continue P2Y12 inhibitor alone.
De-escalation of P2Y12 intensity to clopidogrel at 3 months while continuing aspirin is an option.
Avoid de-escalating DAPT in the first 30 days post-ACS due to increased ischemic risk.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · INVASIVE VERSUS CONSERVATIVE STRATEGY (FIG. 285-4) | LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT

items.title: Mechanism and Clinical Presentation of Vasospastic Angina
items.content: Vasospastic angina (VA) is characterized by focal spasm of an epicardial coronary artery, leading to transmural ischemia. This condition can result in acute myocardial infarction, ventricular arrhythmias, or sudden cardiac death. The spasm may be triggered by hypercontractility due to adrenergic vasoconstrictors, leukotrienes, or serotonin.
keyPoints: VA causes focal spasm of an epicardial coronary artery leading to transmural ischemia.
Can result in acute MI, ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation, and sudden cardiac death.
Spasm may be related to hypercontractility from adrenergic vasoconstrictors, leukotrienes, or serotonin.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · VASOSPASTIC ANGINA

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Vasospastic Angina
items.content: The diagnosis of VA involves detecting transient ST-segment elevation with rest pain. Coronary angiography is used to demonstrate transient coronary spasm, the hallmark of VA. Provocative tests like hyperventilation and intracoronary acetylcholine can induce focal stenosis or angina with ST-segment elevation, aiding diagnosis.
keyPoints: Diagnosis involves detecting transient ST-segment elevation with rest pain.
Coronary angiography shows transient coronary spasm as the diagnostic hallmark.
Provocative tests (e.g., hyperventilation, intracoronary acetylcholine) can induce focal stenosis or angina with ST-segment elevation.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · VASOSPASTIC ANGINA

items.title: Management Strategies for Vasospastic Angina (VA)
items.content: Calcium channel blockers are the primary treatment for VA, while nitroglycerin can relieve acute symptoms. However, long-term use of nitrates is limited by tolerance. Aspirin should be used cautiously due to potential exacerbation of ischemic episodes unless significant atherosclerosis is present. Statins reduce major adverse events risk but their mechanism in VA is unclear. Beta blockers have variable efficacy, and cilostazol or nicorandil may prevent recurrence. Coronary revascularization benefits those with fixed obstructive lesions, and implantable cardioverter-defibrillators are recommended for patients experiencing ischemia-associated ventricular fibrillation despite maximal medical therapy.
keyPoints: Calcium channel blockers are the main therapeutic agents for VA.
Nitroglycerin relieves coronary arterial spasm but has limited long-term efficacy due to nitrate tolerance.
Aspirin may worsen ischemic episodes unless significant atherosclerosis is present.
Statins reduce major adverse events risk, though mechanism in VA is unclear.
Beta blockers have variable response in VA treatment.
Cilostazol and nicorandil can prevent recurrent episodes (nicorandil not available in the U.S.).
Coronary revascularization may benefit patients with fixed obstructive lesions.
Implantable cardioverter-defibrillators recommended for ischemia-associated ventricular fibrillation despite maximal medical therapy.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · Vasospastic Angina | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Prognosis and Risk Factors in Vasospastic Angina (VA)
items.content: Patients with VA often experience an acute phase with frequent angina and cardiac events within the first 6 months, followed by a reduction in symptoms over time. The 5-year survival rate is excellent (~90–95%), but up to 20% may suffer from myocardial infarction (MI). Those without or with mild fixed coronary obstruction have lower cardiac death or MI incidence compared to those with severe obstructive lesions. Patients who develop serious arrhythmias during pain episodes are at higher risk for sudden cardiac events, including infarction.
keyPoints: Acute phase of VA includes frequent angina and cardiac events in the first 6 months.
5-year survival rate is ~90–95%.
Up to 20% of patients experience myocardial infarction (MI).
Lower incidence of cardiac death or MI in patients with no/mild fixed coronary obstruction.
Higher risk for sudden cardiac events, including infarction, in patients developing serious arrhythmias during pain.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · Vasospastic Angina | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: Which of the following is an absolute contraindication to the use of nitrates in anti-ischemic treatment?
items.explanation: The absolute contraindications to the use of nitrates include hypotension or recent use of PDE-5 inhibitors.

items.question: In the context of anti-ischemic treatment, which medication can be used to slow heart rate in patients with contraindications to beta blockers?
items.explanation: Heart rate-slowing calcium channel blockers, such as verapamil or diltiazem, are recommended for patients with contraindications to beta blockers.

items.question: Which of the following agents is recommended for patients who have severe chest pain despite maximal anti-ischemic therapy?
items.explanation: Patients with continuing severe chest pain despite maximal anti-ischemic therapy may receive morphine intravenously.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · Reduced myocardial perfusion | Cardiac conditions | Systemic conditions | Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome | MEDICAL TREATMENT | ANTI-ISCHEMIC TREATMENT (TABLE 285-3)

items.question: What is the recommended initial dose of sublingual nitroglycerin for a patient experiencing ischemic discomfort?
items.explanation: Nitroglycerin should first be given sublingually or by buccal spray at a dose of 0.3–0.6 mg if the patient is experiencing ischemic discomfort.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · Reduced myocardial perfusion | Cardiac conditions | Systemic conditions | Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome | MEDICAL TREATMENT | ANTI-ISCHEMIC TREATMENT (TABLE 285-3)

items.question: Which P2Y12 inhibitor is an inactive prodrug converted into an active metabolite that causes irreversible blockade of the platelet receptor?
items.explanation: Clopidogrel is an inactive prodrug converted into an active metabolite that causes irreversible blockade of the platelet P2Y12 receptor.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: What is a contraindication for prasugrel administration in patients with ACS?
options: Prior stroke or transient ischemic attack
items.explanation: Prasugrel is contraindicated in patients with a prior stroke or transient ischemic attack.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: Which P2Y12 inhibitor has been shown to reduce the risk of cardiovascular death, total mortality, or MI compared to clopidogrel?
items.explanation: Ticagrelor reduces the risk of cardiovascular death, total mortality, or MI compared to clopidogrel.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: What is a potential side effect of ticagrelor that patients may develop soon after administration?
items.explanation: Some patients may develop dyspnea soon after administration of ticagrelor.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: Which parenteral anticoagulant is similar in efficacy to UFH and LMWH, but used specifically prior to or during PCI?
items.explanation: Bivalirudin is a direct thrombin inhibitor used just prior to and/or during PCI.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: Which anticoagulant requires supplemental UFH to prevent procedure-related thrombosis?
items.explanation: Fondaparinux requires supplemental UFH to prevent procedure-related thrombosis.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: In patients with atrial fibrillation undergoing PCI, what is the recommended duration for continuing P2Y12 inhibitor plus DOAC?
items.explanation: P2Y12 inhibitor plus DOAC should be continued for 1 year.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: In patients with NSTE-ACS, which of the following is recommended as part of long-term management to achieve an LDL-C level <55 mg/dL?
options: Intensive lipid-lowering therapies including high-intensity statin, ezetimibe, and possibly a PCSK9 inhibitor or bempedoic acid
items.explanation: Intensive lipid-lowering therapies are recommended to achieve an LDL-C level <55 mg/dL.

items.question: Which antiplatelet regimen is favored over clopidogrel for the initial 12 months in NSTE-ACS management?
options: Ticagrelor or prasugrel combined with low-dose aspirin
items.explanation: Ticagrelor or prasugrel combined with low-dose aspirin is favored over clopidogrel for the initial 12 months.

items.question: In patients at high ischemic risk and low bleeding risk not on anticoagulation, what is the recommended duration for DAPT?
items.explanation: Continuation of DAPT for up to 3 years is beneficial in patients at high ischemic risk and low bleeding risk not on anticoagulation.

items.question: When should aspirin be discontinued in patients requiring long-term anticoagulation after NSTE-ACS?
options: At discharge or within 1 week, followed by oral anticoagulant and clopidogrel until month 12
items.explanation: Aspirin should be discontinued at discharge or within 1 week, followed by oral anticoagulant and clopidogrel until month 12.

items.question: Which of the following substances may be related to the hypercontractility causing vasospastic angina?
items.explanation: Hypercontractility of coronary arterial smooth muscle in vasospastic angina may be related to adrenergic vasoconstrictors, leukotrienes, or serotonin.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · VASOSPASTIC ANGINA

items.question: What is the diagnostic hallmark of vasospastic angina on coronary angiography?
items.explanation: The diagnostic hallmark of vasospastic angina on coronary angiography is transient coronary spasm.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · VASOSPASTIC ANGINA

items.question: Which clinical manifestation is commonly associated with vasospastic angina?
items.explanation: Many patients with vasospastic angina may exhibit episodes of silent ischemia.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · VASOSPASTIC ANGINA

items.question: Which medication is noted to potentially increase the severity of ischemic episodes in vasospastic angina?
items.explanation: Aspirin may increase the severity of ischemic episodes due to its effect on coronary tone.

items.question: What is the long-term efficacy limitation of nitroglycerin in treating vasospastic angina?
options: It leads to increased heart rate
items.explanation: The long-term efficacy of nitroglycerin is limited by the development of nitrate tolerance.

items.question: Which therapy has been shown to reduce the risk of major adverse events in vasospastic angina, though its precise mechanism is not established?
items.explanation: Statin therapy reduces the risk of major adverse events, but the exact mechanism is unknown.

items.statement: Beta blockers should be avoided in patients with hypotension during anti-ischemic treatment.
items.explanation: Beta blockers are to be avoided in the presence of acute or severe heart failure, low cardiac output, and hypotension.

items.statement: Intravenous nitroglycerin infusion can be increased indefinitely until symptoms are relieved.
items.explanation: The intravenous nitroglycerin infusion rate may be increased by 10 μg/min every 3–5 min, but not beyond a dose of 200 μg/min or if systolic arterial pressure falls to <90 mmHg.

items.explanation: Long-term DAPT is preferably continued for 12 months in such patients.

items.statement: In patients with NSTE-ACS treated with oral anticoagulants, aspirin should be stopped at most 1 month after PCI.
items.explanation: Aspirin is recommended to be stopped after hospital discharge or at most 1 month after PCI.

items.explanation: De-escalation of DAPT within the first 30 days is associated with increased risk of ischemic events.

items.statement: Women and racial minorities are more likely to receive evidence-based pharmacologic therapies for NSTE-ACS.
items.explanation: Registries show that women and racial minorities are less likely to receive evidence-based therapies, leading to poorer outcomes.

items.statement: Vasospastic angina is more prevalent in North America than in Japan.
items.explanation: VA remains much more frequent in Japan than in North America or Western Europe.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · VASOSPASTIC ANGINA

items.statement: Patients with vasospastic angina generally have more coronary risk factors compared to those with NSTE-ACS.
items.explanation: Patients with VA are generally younger and, except for cigarette smoking, have fewer coronary risk factors than do patients with NSTE-ACS.

items.reason: Early administration of intensive statins has been shown to reduce both early and late recurrent ischemic events.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.

items.assertion: ACE inhibitors are used to increase heart rate in patients with NSTE-ACS.
items.reason: ACE inhibitors reduce myocardial oxygen needs by lowering blood pressure and afterload.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because ACE inhibitors do not increase heart rate; they help manage blood pressure and afterload.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · Reduced myocardial perfusion | Cardiac conditions | Systemic conditions | Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome | MEDICAL TREATMENT | ANTI-ISCHEMIC TREATMENT (TABLE 285-3)

items.assertion: Prasugrel should be administered after coronary angiography.
items.reason: Prasugrel is contraindicated in patients with prior stroke or transient ischemic attack and should be given once the coronary anatomy is known.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.assertion: Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitors should be used routinely in all patients with NSTE-ACS.
items.reason: Recent studies show no benefit of routine early initiation of glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor when P2Y12 inhibitors are used.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · ANTITHROMBOTIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: INVASIVE VERSUS CONSERVATIVE STRATEGY (FIG. 285-4) | LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT
items.assertion: In patients with NSTE-ACS at high ischemic risk, continuation of DAPT for up to 3 years is beneficial.
items.reason: These patients are also at low risk of bleeding and not on anticoagulation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · INVASIVE VERSUS CONSERVATIVE STRATEGY (FIG. 285-4) | LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: INVASIVE VERSUS CONSERVATIVE STRATEGY (FIG. 285-4) | LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT
items.assertion: In NSTE-ACS management, aspirin should be continued indefinitely.
items.reason: Aspirin is part of the antiplatelet regimen for reducing ischemic events.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; aspirin is not continued indefinitely and is discontinued in certain long-term anticoagulation scenarios.
items.reference: TABLE 285 2 Reasons for the Elevation of Cardiac Troponin Values as a · INVASIVE VERSUS CONSERVATIVE STRATEGY (FIG. 285-4) | LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT
